planting.note: Divide clumps in spring and plant in moist to wet, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade. Florida Wood Fern is adapted to the subtropical conditions of the Florida peninsula and Gulf Coast and is one of the most ornamental evergreen ferns for Zone 8b–10 shade gardens.

directions: Florida Wood Fern is a handsome evergreen native fern of the southeastern coastal plain, native to the moist, shaded understories of cypress swamps, bayheads, and bottomland hardwood forests from South Carolina to peninsular Florida, where it grows in the humid, warm-temperate and subtropical conditions of the lower coastal plain. Its dark, glossy, leathery fronds are fully evergreen in zones 9–11, making it one of the most attractive year-round ferns for warm-climate shade gardens, and its tolerance of briefly flooded conditions suits it to rain garden depressions and pond margins in partial shade. Closely related to Southern Wood Fern (Dryopteris ludoviciana) but adapted to the warmer, more subtropical conditions of the Florida peninsula, it is a better choice than its northern relative for zone 9b–10 landscapes. Divide clumps in spring and plant in deeply amended, consistently moist, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; mulch generously to maintain moisture.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring, anchoring in the muddy bottom of a pond or slow stream at a depth of 1–5 feet. Water Shield spreads by rhizome to form floating mats; provide adequate space in larger ponds where it can naturalize without overpowering other plantings.

directions: Water Shield is a distinctive native floating-leaf aquatic plant found in slow ponds, lakes, bogs, and sluggish streams throughout North America, Asia, and Australia, immediately identifiable by the thick, gelatinous mucilage that coats the undersides of the small, peltate (shield-shaped), olive-green floating leaves and the stems — a characteristic not found in any other native aquatic plant. Small, dark maroon to purplish flowers appear just above the water surface in summer, and the leaves and young stems, with their gelatinous coating, have been used as food in Japan (where it is called junsai) and by Native American tribes. Water Shield is a valuable aquatic plant for wildlife ponds, providing food and cover for frogs, turtles, waterfowl, and invertebrates; the floating leaf mats shade the water and suppress algae. Plant rhizomes in soft mud at the pond bottom at 1–5 feet depth; the plant spreads slowly but steadily in favorable conditions.
sources: Edible Wild Plants of Eastern North America (Fernald & Kinsey)

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun. Necklace Pod is highly salt- and drought-tolerant and is ideal for coastal beach and dune stabilization in zones 8b–11.

directions: Necklace Pod is a striking native coastal shrub, native to the sandy beaches, dunes, and coastal scrub of the Gulf and Atlantic coasts from Florida to Texas, the Caribbean, and tropical Americas, producing long, arching racemes of bright yellow pea-like flowers nearly year-round in frost-free areas that attract hummingbirds, butterflies, and large carpenter bees. The distinctive seed pods are constricted between each seed like a string of beads — giving the plant its common name — and the silvery-gray, velvety, compound leaves provide attractive texture against the beach landscape. One of the finest native plants for coastal stabilization and landscaping in zones 9–11, Necklace Pod tolerates salt spray, poor sandy soil, drought, and heat with equal ease, requiring virtually no care once established in a sunny, well-drained site. Caution: seeds are toxic if ingested.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in partial shade to full sun. Spanish Stopper tolerates salt spray, drought, and partial shade and is an excellent evergreen screening shrub for coastal landscapes in zones 9–11.

directions: Spanish Stopper is an attractive evergreen native shrub or small tree, native to the coastal hammocks, scrub, and pinelands of south Florida and the Florida Keys, with a natural range extending through the Caribbean and tropical Americas. Small, oval, aromatic, dark green leaves densely clothe the branches and produce a thick, fine-textured screen when planted as a hedge; clusters of small white flowers in summer are followed by round black berries that are avidly consumed by mockingbirds, robins, and waxwings. Highly adaptable to a range of coastal conditions including salt spray, drought, and partial shade, Spanish Stopper is an excellent foundation plant, hedge, or specimen for south Florida and Gulf Coast landscapes in zones 9b–11. Plant in well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; established plants require little care and tolerate periodic drought with minimal stress.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun. White Stopper is an excellent native evergreen hedge or specimen plant for south Florida landscapes; it tolerates salt spray, drought, and some shade.

directions: White Stopper is a native evergreen shrub or small tree, native to the coastal hammocks, tropical hardwood forests, and pineland edges of south Florida, the Florida Keys, and the Caribbean, known for its spicy, clove-like or cinnamon fragrance that can be detected from several feet away — the aromatic foliage distinguishes this species from most other Florida stoppers. Clusters of small, white, four-petaled flowers appear in summer and fall, followed by round berries that ripen from red to black and are an important food source for migrating songbirds in fall and winter. It grows to small tree size in protected hammock situations or can be maintained as a dense, evergreen hedge through pruning. White Stopper tolerates partial shade and is one of the most salt-spray-tolerant of the native Eugenia species, making it a versatile choice for coastal gardens in zones 9b–11 that need a fragrant, wildlife-supporting evergreen screen.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade. Wild Coffee is one of the finest native shade shrubs for south Florida landscapes and is an important larval host plant for several native butterfly species.

directions: Wild Coffee is one of the most valuable native shade shrubs for south Florida and tropical hammock gardens, native to the dense, shaded understories of tropical hardwood hammocks, coastal hammocks, and pinelands from Brevard and Hillsborough counties south through the Florida Keys and into the Caribbean. Its large, glossy, strongly quilted or pleated dark green leaves with prominent veins are among the most attractive foliage textures in the Florida native palette, providing a lush, tropical appearance in deep shade where few other shrubs thrive. Small white flowers appear year-round, followed by clusters of bright red berries in fall and winter that are a vital food source for migrating warblers, robins, and thrushes. Despite the common name, it is not a true coffee (Coffea), though the plants share family membership; the berries are not edible by humans. An outstanding shade shrub for zone 9b–11 landscapes that fills the difficult deep-shade role with year-round beauty and wildlife value.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, highly acidic (pH 4.0–5.5), sandy soil in full sun to partial shade. Shiny Blueberry is a low-growing colony-forming blueberry that spreads by rhizome; plant multiple plants or named varieties of the same type for better cross-pollination and heavier fruiting.

directions: Shiny Blueberry is the low-growing native blueberry of the Florida peninsula and southeastern coastal plain, native to the dry, sandy, fire-maintained flatwoods, scrub, and longleaf pine savannas from South Carolina to Florida and west to Mississippi, forming dense, rhizome-spreading colonies under open, sunny conditions. The small, glossy, dark green leaves are evergreen in zones 8–9, giving the plant its common name, and the small, sweet, dark blue berries ripen in spring — earlier than most other blueberries — providing fruit for birds and gardeners alike. Unlike the taller highbush blueberries, Shiny Blueberry is a groundcover-scale plant that works well as an edible lawn substitute in acidic, sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; it requires the same highly acidic soil conditions as all blueberries and benefits from cross-pollination with additional plants for heavy fruiting. Excellent for edible native landscape designs in the lower South.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 4.0–5.5) soil in full sun to partial shade. Elliott's Blueberry is one of the most heat-tolerant native blueberries and performs well in the humid, warm conditions of zones 6–9 in the southeastern coastal plain.

directions: Elliott's Blueberry is one of the most valuable native blueberries for the warm, humid southeastern coastal plain, native to the moist to dry, acidic, sandy soils of pocosins, pine flatwoods, bay swamp edges, and woodland margins from North Carolina to Florida and west to Texas, where it grows in association with other ericaceous plants on acidic, well-drained soils. A vigorous, upright to arching shrub that can reach 8–12 feet in favorable conditions, it produces an abundance of small, sweet, dark blue berries in spring (April–May, depending on location) that ripen earlier than most highbush blueberries and are highly palatable to wildlife and humans alike. One of the most heat- and humidity-tolerant of the native blueberries, it is an excellent choice for the Gulf Coast region where northern highbush varieties struggle; it requires consistently acidic, well-drained soil and benefits from cross-pollination with other Vaccinium species. Plant multiple shrubs for cross-pollination and heavier fruiting; mulch with pine bark or pine needles to maintain soil acidity.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to alkaline, sandy or rocky soil in full sun to partial shade. Florida Privet tolerates salt spray, drought, and alkaline soil, making it ideal for coastal landscapes in zones 8b–11 where other native shrubs struggle.

directions: Florida Privet is a tough, adaptable native shrub, native to coastal hammocks, scrub, and beach margins of south Florida, the Keys, and the Caribbean, where it grows in thin, well-drained, sandy or rocky soils under the influence of salt spray and periodic drought. Related to the ornamental privets used in hedges but native to the Southeast, it produces tiny yellow-green flowers in very early spring (sometimes as early as January) that are among the first nectar sources of the year for native bees, followed by small, dark blue to black oval berries that are important food for migrants and resident birds. An excellent native hedge, screen, or windbreak plant for coastal zones 9–11 where it tolerates salt, alkaline soil, drought, and occasional flooding; male and female plants are needed for berry production, so plant a group for wildlife benefit. Prune as needed for shaping — it is fast-growing and responds well to trimming.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants (male and female plants together) in spring in wet to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade. Swamp Privet is excellent for naturalizing wet bottomlands, streambanks, and seasonally flooded areas throughout zones 5–9.

directions: Swamp Privet is a large, multi-stemmed native deciduous shrub, native to the riverine swamps, oxbow lakes, backwater floodplains, and cypress-tupelo swamps of the southeastern and mid-South United States from South Carolina to Florida and west to Kansas and Texas, tolerating prolonged inundation that few other native shrubs can survive. Inconspicuous yellow flowers appear in February and March before the leaves emerge, providing critical early-season nectar and pollen for native bees emerging from winter dormancy; small, dark blue-purple olive-like drupes ripen in summer and are avidly consumed by wood ducks, mallards, and other water-dependent birds. A valuable plant for the restoration and naturalization of wet bottomland and floodplain habitats, Swamp Privet tolerates prolonged flooding up to 4–6 months and is one of the few native shrubs that can establish in true swamp conditions. Plant male and female individuals together for fruit production; it requires no maintenance once established.
